document: RBI Digital Lending Directions 2025 Digital Lending Guidelines 2022 (India - Row 14)

sections.heading: Preamble
sections.title: Digital lending platform compliance guidelines
sections.summary: This section references RBI's 2020 guidelines on fair practices and outsourcing for loans sourced by banks and NBFCs through digital lending platforms. It establishes baseline compliance requirements for digital lending operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital lending platforms must adhere to fair practices and outsourcing guidelines, impacting data transfer impact assessments for cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Loans sourced by banks and NBFCs through digital lending platforms must adhere to fair practices code
Outsourcing guidelines must be followed for digital lending platforms

sections.source_quote: RBI/2019-20/258 DOR(NBFC)(PD) CC.No.112/03.10.001/2019-20 24.06.2020 Loans Sourced by Banks and NBFCs over Digital Lending Platforms: Adherence to Fair Practices Code and Outsourcing Guidelines

sections.heading: Applicability
sections.title: Applicability of Digital Lending Guidelines
sections.summary: These guidelines apply to all digital lending activities of specified entities: commercial banks, primary (urban) co-operative banks, state co-operative banks, central co-operative banks, non-banking financial companies (including housing finance companies), and all-India financial institutions. The term 'Regulated Entity' refers to these institutions that must comply with the digital lending requirements outlined in the guidelines.

requirements.text: These Directions shall be applicable to all digital lending activities of the following entities
All Commercial Banks are Regulated Entities
All Primary (Urban) Co-operative Banks, State Co-operative Banks, Central Co-operative Banks are Regulated Entities
All Non-Banking Financial Companies (including Housing Finance Companies) are Regulated Entities
All All-India Financial Institutions are Regulated Entities

sections.source_quote: These Directions shall be applicable to all digital lending activities of the following entities, hereinafter referred to as a Regulated Entity (RE) and collectively as Regulated Entities (REs), as the context may require: i. All Commercial Banks, ii. All Primary (Urban) Co-operative Banks, State Co-operative Banks, Central Co-operative Banks, iii. All Non-Banking Financial Companies (including Housing Finance Companies), and iv. All All-India Financial Institutions.

sections.heading: Definitions
sections.title: Definitions for Digital Lending
sections.summary: The guidelines define key terms including Annual Percentage Rate (APR), Default Loss Guarantee (DLG), Digital Lending, Digital Lending Apps/Platforms (DLAs), and Lending Service Providers (LSPs). These definitions clarify the scope of digital lending activities and the roles of entities involved in the lending process, particularly emphasizing the relationship between Regulated Entities and LSPs.

requirements.text: Annual Percentage Rate (APR) is defined under Circular No. DOR.STR.REC.13/13.03.00/2024-25 on 'Key Facts Statement (KFS) for Loans & Advances' dated April 15, 2024
Default Loss Guarantee (DLG) is a contractual arrangement between RE and another entity for compensating RE for loan portfolio losses
Digital Lending is a remote and automated lending process using digital technologies for customer acquisition, credit assessment, loan approval, disbursement, recovery and customer service
Digital Lending Apps/Platforms (DLAs) are mobile and/or web-based applications facilitating digital lending services
Lending Service Provider (LSP) is an agent of a RE who carries out one or more digital lending functions

sections.source_quote: For the purpose of these Directions, unless the context otherwise requires, the terms herein shall bear the meaning assigned to them below: i. Annual Percentage Rate (APR): APR as defined under Circular No. DOR.STR.REC.13/13.03.00/2024-25 on 'Key Facts Statement (KFS) for Loans & Advances' dated April 15, 2024, as amended from time to time.

sections.heading: Due diligence requirements with respect to LSPs
sections.title: Due Diligence Requirements for LSPs
sections.summary: Regulated Entities must conduct enhanced due diligence before entering agreements with Lending Service Providers, including assessing technical capabilities, data privacy policies, robustness of storage systems, fairness in conduct with borrowers, past records, and compliance with regulations. They must also carry out periodic reviews of LSP conduct, establish monitoring mechanisms for loan portfolios, provide guidance to LSPs on recovery duties, and ensure compliance with outsourcing guidelines. The RE remains fully responsible for all acts and omissions of the LSP.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulated Entities must ensure LSPs meet strict due diligence standards before partnership, conduct periodic reviews, and maintain oversight to prevent breaches of data privacy and borrower protection. This section directly impacts data transfer impact assessments by requiring robust third-party vendor management for digital lending activities.

requirements.text: Digital lending by a RE involving a LSP shall be carried out under a contractual agreement defining respective roles, rights, and obligations

RE shall conduct enhanced due diligence before entering agreement with LSP considering technical capabilities, data privacy policies, storage systems, fairness with borrowers, past records, and compliance
RE shall carry out periodic review of LSP conduct against contractual terms and take action for deviations
RE shall lay down suitable monitoring mechanisms for loan portfolios originated with LSP support
RE shall impart necessary guidance to LSP acting as recovery agent to discharge duties responsibly
RE shall continue to conform to extant guidelines on outsourcing and ensure LSPs and DLAs comply with these Directions
Any outsourcing agreement with LSP shall not dilute or absolve RE of statutory or regulatory obligations

sections.source_quote: Digital lending by a RE involving a LSP, shall be carried out under a contractual agreement between the RE and the LSP, which clearly defines the respective roles, rights, and obligations of each party thereto.

sections.heading: RE-LSP arrangements involving multiple lenders
sections.title: Multi-Lender Arrangements with LSPs
sections.summary: When a Lending Service Provider has agreements with multiple Regulated Entities, each RE must ensure the LSP provides a digital view of loan offers matching the borrower's request, including unmatched lenders. The LSP must follow a consistent approach for similarly placed borrowers, document mechanisms, and display loan offers with unbiased content that includes lender names, loan amounts, tenors, APR, repayment obligations, and penal charges. The content must not promote specific REs through dark patterns or deceptive practices.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires LSPs to provide transparent, unbiased loan comparisons across lenders, directly impacting data transfer impact assessments by mandating clear disclosure mechanisms for cross-entity data sharing and preventing misleading practices in digital lending.

requirements.text: LSP shall provide digital view of all loan offers matching borrower's request on DLA
LSP shall disclose unmatched lenders in the digital view
LSP shall follow consistent approach for similarly placed borrowers and products
LSP shall document mechanisms adopted for matching borrowers with lenders
Digital view of loan offers shall include lender names, loan amounts, tenors, APR, monthly repayment obligations and penal charges
Digital view shall provide link to Key Facts Statement (KFS) for each RE
Content displayed by LSP shall be unbiased, objective and not promote specific REs through dark patterns

sections.source_quote: LSP shall provide a digital view of all the loan offers matching the borrower's request on the DLA which meets the requirement of the borrower. The name of the unmatched lenders shall also be disclosed in the digital view.

sections.heading: Assessing the borrower's creditworthiness
sections.title: Borrower Creditworthiness Assessment
sections.summary: Regulated Entities must obtain necessary information about borrowers' economic profiles to assess creditworthiness before extending loans, including minimum age, occupation, and income details. These details must be kept on record for audit purposes. Additionally, REs must ensure that credit limits cannot be automatically increased without explicit borrower requests, evaluations, and records.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts data transfer impact assessments by requiring detailed borrower information collection and strict controls on credit limit increases, ensuring data accuracy and compliance with financial regulations.

requirements.text: RE shall obtain necessary information relating to borrower's economic profile for creditworthiness assessment
RE shall keep age, occupation and income details on record for audit purposes
RE shall ensure no automatic increase in credit limit without explicit borrower request, evaluation and record

sections.source_quote: RE shall obtain the necessary information relating to economic profile of the borrower with a view to assessing the borrower's creditworthiness before extending any loan, including, at a minimum, age, occupation and income details.

sections.heading: Disclosures to borrowers
sections.title: Borrower disclosures and document delivery
sections.summary: RBI requires digital lenders (REs) to provide borrowers with a Key Fact Statement (KFS) following specific circulars. REs must deliver digitally signed documents including KFS, loan product summaries, sanction letters, and privacy policies automatically to borrowers via email or SMS upon contract execution. REs must maintain a public website with detailed information about lending products, third-party service providers (LSPs), grievance mechanisms, and links to RBI's complaint systems. REs must also notify borrowers of recovery agents before contact for loan defaults.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates direct delivery of key documents to borrowers via email/SMS, requires public disclosure of lending products and LSPs, and specifies data sharing protocols for recovery agents — critical for ensuring transparency and compliance in cross-border data transfers involving Indian digital lenders.

requirements.text: RE shall provide Key Fact Statement (KFS) per circular DOR.STR.REC.13/13.03.00/2024-25
RE shall ensure digitally signed documents (KFS, loan product summary, sanction letter, etc.) automatically flow to borrowers via email/SMS upon contract execution
RE shall maintain public website with lending products, LSP details, grievance mechanisms, and links to RBI's complaint systems
RE shall ensure DLAs/LSPs link to RE's public website
RE shall notify borrowers of recovery agents via email/SMS before contact for loan defaults

sections.source_quote: RE shall provide a Key Fact Statement (KFS) as per instructions contained in circular no. DOR.STR.REC.13/13.03.00/2024-25 on ‘Key Facts Statement (KFS) for Loans & Advances' dated April 15, 2024, as amended from time to time.

sections.heading: Loan disbursal, servicing and repayment
sections.title: Loan disbursal and repayment without third-party intermediaries
sections.summary: Digital lenders must disburse loans directly to borrowers' bank accounts, excluding third-party accounts including LSPs, except for statutory mandates, co-lending transactions, or specific end-use cases. All loan servicing and repayments must be executed directly by borrowers in the lender's bank account without pass-through accounts. Funds flow between borrowers and lenders must not be controlled by third parties, and fees payable to LSPs must be directly handled by the lender without charging borrowers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires direct bank account transfers for loans, prohibits third-party fund control, and mandates lender responsibility for LSP fees — essential for ensuring data flows remain within India and avoid cross-border data transfer risks.

requirements.text: Loan disbursement must be made into borrower's bank account except for statutory mandates, co-lending transactions, or specific end-use cases
All loan servicing and repayments must be executed directly in RE's bank account without third-party pass-through accounts
Funds flow between borrower and RE must not be controlled by third parties including LSPs
Fees payable to LSPs must be paid directly by RE without being charged to borrowers

sections.source_quote: RE shall ensure that in no case, disbursal is made to a third-party account, including the accounts of LSP, except as provided for in these Directions.

sections.heading: Cooling-off period
sections.title: Cooling-off period for loan exits
sections.summary: Borrowers must be given a cooling-off period during which they can exit a digital loan by repaying principal and proportionate APR without penalty. The period must be at least one day and determined by the RE's board. REs may retain a one-time processing fee if borrowers exit during this period, but this must be disclosed upfront in the KFS.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires a minimum one-day cooling-off period for loan exits, with clear exit mechanisms — relevant for data transfer impact assessments as it affects borrower consent and data retention timelines.

requirements.text: Borrower must have explicit option to exit loan by repaying principal and proportionate APR without penalty during cooling-off period
Cooling-off period must be at least one day and determined by RE's board
RE may retain one-time processing fee if borrower exits during cooling-off period, disclosed upfront in KFS

sections.source_quote: The borrower shall be given an explicit option to exit a digital loan by paying the principal and the proportionate APR without any penalty during an initial 'cooling-off period'.

sections.heading: Grievance redressal
sections.title: Grievance redressal mechanisms
sections.summary: Digital lenders and their LSPs must designate nodal grievance redressal officers for borrower complaints. Contact details must be prominently displayed on websites, DLAs, and KFS. Borrowers can escalate complaints to RBI's CMS portal if unresolved within 30 days, with RE retaining responsibility for grievance resolution.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates clear grievance channels and RBI escalation pathways — critical for data transfer impact assessments as it ensures accountability for data-related complaints across borders.

requirements.text: RE and LSP must designate nodal grievance redressal officers for digital lending complaints
Contact details of nodal officers must be prominently displayed on RE's website, LSPs, and DLA
Borrowers can escalate complaints to RBI's CMS portal if unresolved within 30 days

sections.source_quote: The RE, and the LSP which has an interface with the borrower, shall designate nodal grievance redressal officers to deal with digital lending related complaints/issues raised by the borrower.

sections.heading: Collection, usage and sharing of data with third parties
sections.title: Data consent and third-party sharing
sections.summary: Digital lenders must collect data need-based with explicit consent and audit trail. They must not access mobile phone resources like contacts or call logs, except for one-time access to camera/microphone/location for KYC. Borrowers must be given options to manage consent, restrict third-party sharing, and delete data. Explicit consent is required before sharing personal information with third parties, except for statutory mandates.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit consent for data collection and sharing, with strict limitations on mobile data access — directly impacts data transfer impact assessments by defining consent mechanisms and third-party data flows.

requirements.text: RE must ensure data collection is need-based with prior and explicit consent and audit trail
RE must not access mobile phone resources like contacts, call logs, etc., except for one-time access to camera/microphone/location for KYC
Borrowers must be provided options to manage consent, restrict third-party sharing, and delete data
Explicit consent required before sharing personal information with third parties, except for statutory mandates

sections.source_quote: RE shall ensure that any collection of data by their DLA and DLA of their LSP is need-based and with prior and explicit consent of the borrower having audit trail.

sections.heading: Storage of data
sections.title: Data storage and localization requirements
sections.summary: Digital lenders must ensure LSPs store only minimal borrower data (name, address, contact details) and retain responsibility for data privacy and security. Clear policies on data storage duration, usage restrictions, and destruction protocols must be disclosed. Biometric data storage is prohibited unless permitted by statutory guidelines. All data must be stored within India, with data processed outside India deleted within 24 hours and brought back to India.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates data localization within India, strict storage duration limits, and immediate data re-transfer requirements for cross-border processing — critical for data transfer impact assessments involving international data flows.

requirements.text: LSPs may store only minimal borrower data (name, address, contact details) for operational purposes
RE must retain responsibility for data privacy and security of borrower information
Clear policies on data storage duration, usage restrictions, and destruction protocols must be disclosed
Biometric data storage prohibited unless permitted by statutory guidelines
All data must be stored only in servers within India
Data processed outside India must be deleted within 24 hours and brought back to India

sections.source_quote: RE shall ensure that all data is stored only in servers located within India, while ensuring compliance with statutory obligations/regulatory instructions.

sections.heading: Comprehensive privacy policy
sections.title: Public availability of comprehensive privacy policy
sections.summary: Retail Entities (REs) and LSPs must maintain a publicly accessible privacy policy compliant with laws, regulations, and RBI guidelines. The policy must detail third parties involved in collecting personal information through Digital Lending Arrangements (DLAs). This applies to all REs and their LSPs engaged in digital lending activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires transparency in data handling practices for cross-border transfers by mandating public disclosure of privacy policies and third-party data collection details.

requirements.text: RE and LSPs must have a comprehensive privacy policy compliant with applicable laws, regulations, and RBI guidelines
Privacy policy must be made publicly available on RE or LSP websites
Details of third parties allowed to collect personal information through DLAs must be disclosed in the privacy policy

sections.source_quote: RE and LSPs engaged by RE shall have a comprehensive privacy policy compliant with applicable laws, associated regulations and RBI guidelines which shall be made available publicly on the website of RE and LSP, as the case may be.

sections.heading: Technology standards
sections.title: Cybersecurity technology standards for digital lending
sections.summary: Retail Entities must ensure their digital lending platforms and third-party LSPs comply with RBI and other relevant agencies' cybersecurity standards. This includes implementing necessary technology standards for secure digital lending operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets cybersecurity requirements for digital lending platforms, impacting data security during cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: RE and LSPs must comply with technology standards for cybersecurity stipulated by RBI and other relevant agencies

sections.source_quote: RE shall ensure that they and the LSPs engaged by them comply with various technology standards/ requirements on cybersecurity stipulated by RBI and other relevant agencies, or as may be specified from time to time, for undertaking digital lending.

sections.heading: Reporting to Credit Information Companies (CICs)
sections.title: Mandatory reporting of digital lending to credit information companies
sections.summary: Retail Entities must report all digital lending activities to Credit Information Companies (CICs) regardless of loan type or duration. This includes reporting deferred payment credit products and ensuring LSPs comply with outsourcing guidelines when involved in such products.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires reporting of digital lending data to CICs, which affects data sharing and cross-border data flows for credit information.

requirements.text: RE must report all lending done through DLAs to CICs irrespective of loan nature or tenor
RE must ensure LSPs comply with outsourcing guidelines for deferred payment credit products

sections.source_quote: RE shall ensure that any lending done through their DLAs and/or DLAs of LSPs is reported by them to CICs irrespective of its nature/ tenor.

sections.heading: Reporting of DLAs to RBI
sections.title: Reporting of digital lending arrangements to RBI
sections.summary: Retail Entities must report all deployed Digital Lending Arrangements (DLAs) to RBI's CIMS portal, including updates when new DLAs are deployed or existing ones cease. The Chief Compliance Officer must certify that DLAs comply with regulations and are correctly reported.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed reporting of digital lending infrastructure to RBI, impacting data residency and cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: RE must report all DLAs deployed or joined by them on RBI's CIMS portal
RE must update CIMS with new DLAs or discontinued ones
Chief Compliance Officer must certify that DLA data on CIMS is correct and compliant
DLAs must link to RE's website with loan product details, customer care, privacy policies, etc.
DLAs (if owned by LSP) must have a nodal grievance redressal officer
DLAs must comply with data collection and storage requirements
DLA details must be prominently disclosed on RE's website
RE must ensure timely reporting of DLAs by June 15, 2025

sections.source_quote: RE shall report all DLAs deployed/ joined by them, whether their own or those of the LSPs, either exclusively or as a platform participant, on the Centralised Withdrawn Information Management System (CIMS) portal of RBI in the requisite format as given in the Annex-I to these Directions.

sections.heading: Due diligence and other requirements with respect to DLG provider
sections.title: Due diligence for digital lending guarantee providers
sections.summary: Retail Entities must establish a Board-approved policy before entering into Digital Lending Guarantee (DLG) arrangements. The policy must include eligibility criteria, DLG coverage details, monitoring processes, and fee structures. RE must ensure DLG providers can honor commitments and maintain robust credit underwriting standards.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires due diligence on DLG providers, impacting risk assessment and data sharing with third parties.

requirements.text: RE must have a Board-approved policy before entering DLG arrangements
Policy must include eligibility criteria for DLG providers
Policy must detail nature and extent of DLG cover
Policy must specify monitoring and review processes for DLG arrangements
Policy must include details of fees payable or received by DLG providers
RE must ensure DLG arrangements do not substitute credit appraisal requirements
RE must obtain information to verify DLG provider's ability to honor commitments
DLG provider must submit a certified declaration on outstanding DLG amounts, number of REs, portfolios, and past default rates

sections.source_quote: RE, including a RE acting as DLG provider, shall put in place a Board approved policy before entering into any DLG arrangement.

sections.heading: Restrictions on entering into DLG arrangements
sections.title: Restrictions on digital lending guarantee arrangements
sections.summary: Retail Entities cannot enter into DLG arrangements for revolving credit facilities through digital lending channels or credit cards. They also cannot enter into DLG arrangements for loans covered by credit guarantee schemes administered by trust funds. NBFC-P2P entities are prohibited from entering into DLG arrangements for loans facilitated over their platforms.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets specific restrictions on DLG arrangements, affecting data transfer and processing for certain financial products.

requirements.text: RE shall not enter into DLG arrangements for revolving credit facilities offered through digital lending channel and credit cards
RE shall not enter into DLG arrangements for loans covered by credit guarantee schemes administered by trust funds
NBFC-P2P shall not enter into DLG arrangements for loans facilitated over their platform

sections.source_quote: RE shall not enter into DLG arrangements for revolving credit facilities offered through digital lending channel and credit cards as defined under Master Direction – Credit Card and Debit Card – Issuance and Conduct Directions, 2022 dated April 21, 2022.

sections.heading: Treatment of DLG for regulatory capital
sections.title: Regulatory capital treatment for digital lending guarantees
sections.summary: This section mandates that capital computation for individual loan assets under digital lending guarantees (DLGs) continues using existing norms. DLG providers that are REs (Reserve Enterprises) must deduct the full outstanding DLG amount from their capital. The section specifies that capital calculations for credit risk mitigation must follow extant regulatory frameworks.
sections.dtia_summary: DLG arrangements must be deducted from capital for REs, impacting capital adequacy calculations for financial institutions engaged in digital lending.

requirements.text: Capital computation for individual loan assets under DLG must follow extant norms
REs must deduct full outstanding DLG amount from capital

sections.source_quote: Capital computation, i.e., computation of exposure and application of Credit Risk Mitigation benefits on individual loan assets in the portfolio shall continue to be governed by the extant norms.

sections.heading: Disclosure requirements
sections.title: Monthly disclosure of DLG portfolio details to public
sections.summary: REs must publish monthly on their website the total number of portfolios and respective amounts of each portfolio covered by DLG. The RE's name may or may not be disclosed. Disclosure must be completed within seven working days after the month ends. This section establishes transparency requirements for DLG arrangements.
sections.dtia_summary: REs must publicly disclose DLG portfolio details monthly, creating transparency requirements for data transfer impact assessments involving digital lending guarantees.

requirements.text: REs must publish monthly on website total portfolios and respective DLG amounts
RE name may or may not be disclosed in public disclosures
Monthly disclosures must be completed within seven working days after month ends

sections.source_quote: RE shall put in place a mechanism to ensure that LSPs with whom they have a DLG arrangement shall publish on their website the total number of portfolios and the respective amount of each portfolio on which DLG has been offered.

sections.heading: Exceptions
sections.title: Exclusions for DLG coverage under specific schemes
sections.summary: Guarantees covered under specific schemes like CGTMSE, CRGFTLIH, NCGTC, BIS, IMF, and Multilateral Development Banks are excluded from DLG coverage. This section defines which guarantees do not qualify as DLG under the regulatory framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Certain guarantees (e.g., government-backed schemes) are excluded from DLG coverage, reducing the scope of data transfer impact assessments for these specific instruments.

requirements.text: CGTMSE, CRGFTLIH, and NCGTC schemes are excluded from DLG coverage
BIS, IMF, and Multilateral Development Bank guarantees are excluded from DLG coverage

sections.source_quote: Guarantees covered under the following schemes/ entities shall not be covered within the definition of DLG

sections.heading: General provisions
sections.title: Special provisions for credit card EMIs and DLG arrangements
sections.summary: EMI programs on credit cards are governed by specific Master Directions and not covered under DLG. Other credit card loans fall under DLG. DLG arrangements conforming to Chapter VI instructions are not treated as synthetic securitisation or loan participation. These Directions do not override other extant regulatory norms.
sections.dtia_summary: DLG arrangements must comply with specific credit card EMIs and securitisation rules, affecting data transfer impact assessments for financial institutions using credit card-based lending.

requirements.text: Credit card EMI programs are governed by specific Master Directions and not covered under DLG
DLG arrangements conforming to Chapter VI instructions are not treated as synthetic securitisation
DLG arrangements conforming to Chapter VI instructions do not attract loan participation provisions
These Directions do not override other extant regulatory norms

sections.source_quote: DLG arrangements entered between RE and LSP conforming to the instructions laid down in Chapter VI of these Directions shall not be treated as synthetic securitisation

sections.heading: Repeal provisions
sections.title: Repeal of previous circulars and transitional provisions
sections.summary: The Directions repeal specific circulars mentioned in Annex III. Actions taken under repealed circulars remain valid if not inconsistent with these Directions. This section handles the transition from previous regulations to the new framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Repeal provisions ensure continuity of regulatory compliance for financial institutions transitioning from old to new DLG frameworks.

requirements.text: Circulars listed in Annex III are repealed with issuance of these Directions
Actions under repealed circulars remain valid if not inconsistent with these Directions

sections.source_quote: With the issuance of these Directions, the instructions/guidelines contained in the circulars mentioned in the Annex - III issued by RBI stand repealed.

sections.heading: RBI/2019-20/258
sections.title: Digital lending platform compliance guidelines
sections.summary: This section references RBI's 2020 guidelines on fair practices and outsourcing for loans sourced by banks and NBFCs through digital lending platforms. It establishes baseline compliance requirements for digital lending operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Digital lending platforms must adhere to fair practices and outsourcing guidelines, impacting data transfer impact assessments for cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Loans sourced by banks and NBFCs through digital lending platforms must adhere to fair practices code
Outsourcing guidelines must be followed for digital lending platforms

sections.source_quote: RBI/2019-20/258 DOR(NBFC)(PD) CC.No.112/03.10.001/2019-20 24.06.2020 Loans Sourced by Banks and NBFCs over Digital Lending Platforms: Adherence to Fair Practices Code and Outsourcing Guidelines
